description.task: Implement Kruskal's MST algorithm using Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union). Sort all edges by weight, then greedily add the smallest edge that doesn't form a cycle.
instructions: Then iterate: for each edge {u,v,w}, if union(u,v) is true, add to mst list
Stop early when mst.size()==V-1 if you prefer (optional optimization)
MST includes: 2-3(4), 0-3(5), 0-1(10) = total 19; edge 0-2 skipped (would cycle)

description.task: Implement Prim's MST algorithm using a min-heap PriorityQueue. Start from vertex 0, and greedily pick the minimum weight edge connecting a visited vertex to an unvisited one.
instructions: Iterate over adj[u]: for(int[] edge : adj[u]){ int v=edge[0], ew=edge[1]; ... }
If !inMST[v] && ew < key[v]: set key[v]=ew and pq.offer(new int[]{ew,v})
The MST weight is 4+5+10=19 (same result as Kruskal for the same graph)
The inMST check at pop prevents reprocessing a vertex
Do not modify the structure or variable names
description.hint: for(int[] edge:adj[u]){int v=edge[0],ew=edge[1]; if(!inMST[v]&&ew<key[v]){key[v]=ew; pq.offer(new int[]{ew,v});}}

description.task: Create a class MSTAnalysis that computes MST weight using both Kruskal's and Prim's algorithms and verifies they produce the same total weight. Also count the number of edges in the MST.
instructions: All implementations are provided — run the code and analyze the output
An MST of V vertices always has exactly V-1 edges
Both Kruskal and Prim produce the same total MST weight for the same graph
Observe that Kruskal sorts edges globally while Prim grows the MST from a seed vertex
The main method demonstrates how both algorithms confirm MST weight = 19
description.hint: This is a read-and-run exercise. Ensure you understand why both algorithms give the same MST weight, and note that MST edge count is always V-1.
description.expectedOutput:
Kruskal MST weight: 19
Prim MST weight: 19
Same result: true
MST edge count: 3
